_article: XLI §4 — Familiarity Is A Relationship Dimension
_description: Multi-dimensional relationship weights. The existing relationship graph (Article XXXIV) uses a single 'strength' float per edge. This schema defines the six required dimensions that replace that single value. Each dimension is 0.0-1.0.
_key_insight: A legendary elder you have heard of but never met: Respect 100, Familiarity 5. A village merchant you see daily: Trust 15, Familiarity 95. These produce radically different conversations. The same words from each feel nothing alike.

trust.meaning: Belief the other will act in your interest. Built through reliable positive interactions. Broken by betrayal.
trust.growth_rate: slow — years of consistent behavior required
trust.decay_rate: rapid — one betrayal can destroy years of trust
trust.affects_conversation: High trust: direct speech, fewer formalities, willingness to ask for help. Low trust: guarded speech, evasive answers, reluctance to share information.

respect.meaning: Acknowledgment of strength, character, or wisdom. Can exist without trust (a respected enemy) or familiarity (a legendary elder heard of but never met).
respect.decay_rate: slow — respect is durable once earned
respect.affects_conversation: High respect: deferential language, willingness to follow advice, acceptance of criticism. Low respect: dismissive tone, challenges, refusal to take seriously.

fear.meaning: Perceived threat differential. Based on power gap, not morality. You can fear someone you respect (a strict teacher) or someone you hate (a powerful enemy).
fear.growth_rate: instant — power displays create fear immediately
fear.decay_rate: moderate — familiarity and equal power reduce fear over time
fear.affects_conversation: High fear: submissive speech, agreement regardless of opinion, physical retreat. Low fear: relaxed posture, disagreement, eye contact.

familiarity.meaning: Repeated exposure has made this person a known quantity. Not friendship. Not trust. Simply: they are part of my world. Day 1: 0. Day 20: nods. Day 60: 'You are late.' They never invited you, but somehow they expected you.
familiarity.growth_rate: slow and steady — proximity and repeated interaction over time
familiarity.decay_rate: very slow — familiarity persists even after long absence
familiarity.affects_conversation: High familiarity: shortened speech, shared references, unspoken understanding, absence of introductions. Low familiarity: full introductions, formal address, explanation of context. The advisor's example: Wang Lin Day 1 says 'Who are you?' Day 20: nods. Day 60: 'You are late.' — all driven by familiarity, not trust or respect.

_canon_examples.context: Wang Lin and the player, early in the novel
_canon_examples.behavior: Wang Lin does not acknowledge the player. The player is a stranger. No interaction.

_canon_examples.context: After 20 days of proximity (player keeps returning to where Wang Lin cultivates)
_canon_examples.behavior: Wang Lin nods when the player passes. No words. But the nod means: I know you exist.

_canon_examples.context: After 60 days (player has been consistently present)
_canon_examples.behavior: Wang Lin says 'You are late.' without looking up. He never invited the player. But the player has become part of his world. This is familiarity, not friendship.

_canon_examples.context: Old Chen and the village merchant, years of daily interaction
_canon_examples.behavior: Conversations without greeting. Shared jokes. References to events both remember. The kind of interaction that only comes from years of proximity.

debt.meaning: Obligation (karmic, social, or material). Can be one-directional (A owes B) or mutual. In Er Gen, karmic debt is a physical law.
debt.decay_rate: varies — karmic debt decays slowly, social debt decays with reciprocity
debt.affects_conversation: High debt: requests for repayment, guilt-driven compliance, avoidance. The debtor is uncomfortable; the creditor has leverage.

grievance.meaning: Unresolved harm. Accumulates from injuries, slights, and broken promises. In Er Gen, grievances fester and can last lifetimes across reincarnations.
grievance.decay_rate: very slow without resolution — grievances fester for centuries in canon
grievance.affects_conversation: High grievance: coldness, passive aggression, refusal to cooperate, plotting. In Er Gen, a grievance of 0.8+ can persist through multiple reincarnations and drive multi-generational conflicts.

_example_profiles.description: A legendary elder you have heard stories about but never met
A village merchant you see daily
Wang Lin, early novel (player first encounters him)

_integration_with_existing_graph.EdgeType: Add FAMILIAR_WITH as a new social edge type in EdgeType.java. This edge is created automatically when familiarity exceeds 0.1 between two actors who have co-existed for N ticks. It is not created by events — it emerges from proximity over time.
_integration_with_existing_graph.Edge_properties: Each edge in the relationship graph gains a 'dimensions' property containing the six floats. The existing 'strength' field is deprecated in favor of these dimensions. For backward compatibility, strength = weighted average of dimensions if dimensions are absent.
_integration_with_existing_graph.WorldGraph_queries: New query methods: relationshipDimensions(actorA, actorB) -> dimensions object. familiarWith(actorA, threshold) -> [actor]. The graph can now answer 'who does this character actually know well?' versus 'who is this character connected to?'
